- Evidence of IR coordination for physical security incidents

column1: PE-06 (01): Does your facility use intrusion alarms and surveillance cameras to monitor physical access?
column8:
**Control:** PE-06(01) — Intrusion Alarms and Surveillance Equipment

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
PE-06(01): Monitor physical access to the facility where the system resides using physical intrusion alarms and surveillance equipment.

**Note for AWS-hosted systems:** If your system is fully hosted in AWS with no on-premises infrastructure, all PE controls are inherited from AWS under their FedRAMP P-ATO. Document this inheritance by downloading the AWS Customer Responsibility Matrix from AWS Artifact and writing a one-page SSP appendix.

**Suggested evidence:**
- CCTV and alarm system documentation
- AWS Artifact (if inherited)

column1: PE-08 a: Are visitor access records kept for at least one year?
column8:
**Control:** PE-08 — Visitor Access Records

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
PE-08: (a) Maintain visitor access records for a defined time period; (b) review visitor access records at a defined frequency; and (c) report anomalies to defined personnel.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement a):** [Assignment resolved] Retention period = at least 1 year.

**Note for AWS-hosted systems:** If your system is fully hosted in AWS with no on-premises infrastructure, all PE controls are inherited from AWS under their FedRAMP P-ATO. Document this inheritance by downloading the AWS Customer Responsibility Matrix from AWS Artifact and writing a one-page SSP appendix.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Visitor log (physical sign-in book or electronic system)
- Visitor log review records
- AWS Artifact (if inherited)

column1: PE-08 b: Are visitor access records reviewed monthly for anomalies?
column8:
**Control:** PE-08 — Visitor Access Records

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
PE-08: (a) Maintain visitor access records for a defined time period; (b) review visitor access records at a defined frequency; and (c) report anomalies to defined personnel.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement b):** [Assignment resolved] Review frequency = monthly.

**Note for AWS-hosted systems:** If your system is fully hosted in AWS with no on-premises infrastructure, all PE controls are inherited from AWS under their FedRAMP P-ATO. Document this inheritance by downloading the AWS Customer Responsibility Matrix from AWS Artifact and writing a one-page SSP appendix.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Visitor log (physical sign-in book or electronic system)
- Visitor log review records
- AWS Artifact (if inherited)

column1: PE-08 c: If anomalies are found in visitor records (unexpected visits, after-hours access), are they reported to the security team?
column8:
**Control:** PE-08 — Visitor Access Records

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
PE-08: (a) Maintain visitor access records for a defined time period; (b) review visitor access records at a defined frequency; and (c) report anomalies to defined personnel.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement c):** [Assignment resolved] Reporting personnel = facility security officer or physical security team.

**Note for AWS-hosted systems:** If your system is fully hosted in AWS with no on-premises infrastructure, all PE controls are inherited from AWS under their FedRAMP P-ATO. Document this inheritance by downloading the AWS Customer Responsibility Matrix from AWS Artifact and writing a one-page SSP appendix.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Visitor log (physical sign-in book or electronic system)
- Visitor log review records
- AWS Artifact (if inherited)

column1: PE-11: Is there an uninterruptible power supply (UPS) or backup power source in place to keep the system running or enable an orderly shutdown if primary power is lost?
column8:
**Control:** PE-11 — Emergency Power

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
PE-11: Provide an uninterruptible power supply to facilitate an orderly shutdown of the system or transition to long-term alternate power in the event of a primary power source loss.

**GovRAMP Parameter:** [Assignment resolved] For cloud-hosted systems on AWS: this is fully inherited from AWS. AWS data centers use redundant UPS systems and diesel generators with automatic failover.

**Note for AWS-hosted systems:** If your system is fully hosted in AWS with no on-premises infrastructure, all PE controls are inherited from AWS under their FedRAMP P-ATO. Document this inheritance by downloading the AWS Customer Responsibility Matrix from AWS Artifact and writing a one-page SSP appendix.

**Suggested evidence:**
- AWS Artifact or FedRAMP P-ATO reference for PE-11 inheritance
- UPS and generator documentation (if on-premises components exist)

column1: RA-05 a: Does your organization run vulnerability scans monthly for infrastructure and quarterly for web applications — and also when new vulnerabilities are announced?
column8:
**Control:** RA-05 — Vulnerability Monitoring and Scanning

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
RA-05: (a) Monitor and scan for vulnerabilities at a defined frequency and when new vulnerabilities are identified; (b) employ vulnerability scanning tools that enumerate platforms, software flaws, and improper configurations; (c) analyze vulnerability scan reports; (d) remediate legitimate vulnerabilities within defined response times; (e) share vulnerability information with defined personnel; and (f) employ tools that can update the vulnerability database.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement a):** [Assignment resolved] Frequency = monthly for OS and infrastructure components; quarterly for web applications. Also scan when new vulnerabilities are identified that may affect in-scope components.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Vulnerability scan results (last 30 days)
- Scan schedule and tool configuration
- Vulnerability remediation tracker (POA&M or ticket system)
- Scan tool update/signature verification

column1: RA-05 b-1: Does your vulnerability scanning tool identify specific vulnerabilities across platforms, missing patches, and misconfigurations?
column8:
**Control:** RA-05 — Vulnerability Monitoring and Scanning

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
RA-05: (a) Monitor and scan for vulnerabilities at a defined frequency and when new vulnerabilities are identified; (b) employ vulnerability scanning tools that enumerate platforms, software flaws, and improper configurations; (c) analyze vulnerability scan reports; (d) remediate legitimate vulnerabilities within defined response times; (e) share vulnerability information with defined personnel; and (f) employ tools that can update the vulnerability database.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement b-1):** Scanning tools must enumerate specific CVEs, missing patches, and configuration weaknesses — not just report pass/fail. Authenticated scans are required.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Vulnerability scan results (last 30 days)
- Scan schedule and tool configuration
- Vulnerability remediation tracker (POA&M or ticket system)
- Scan tool update/signature verification

column1: RA-05 b-2: Does your scanning tool use current vulnerability checklists and test procedures?
column8:
**Control:** RA-05 — Vulnerability Monitoring and Scanning

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
RA-05: (a) Monitor and scan for vulnerabilities at a defined frequency and when new vulnerabilities are identified; (b) employ vulnerability scanning tools that enumerate platforms, software flaws, and improper configurations; (c) analyze vulnerability scan reports; (d) remediate legitimate vulnerabilities within defined response times; (e) share vulnerability information with defined personnel; and (f) employ tools that can update the vulnerability database.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement b-2):** Vulnerability signatures must be updated before each scan. Tools such as Tenable, Qualys, or AWS Inspector automatically update their vulnerability databases.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Vulnerability scan results (last 30 days)
- Scan schedule and tool configuration
- Vulnerability remediation tracker (POA&M or ticket system)
- Scan tool update/signature verification

column1: RA-05 b-3: Does your scanning tool assign severity ratings to identified vulnerabilities?
column8:
**Control:** RA-05 — Vulnerability Monitoring and Scanning

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
RA-05: (a) Monitor and scan for vulnerabilities at a defined frequency and when new vulnerabilities are identified; (b) employ vulnerability scanning tools that enumerate platforms, software flaws, and improper configurations; (c) analyze vulnerability scan reports; (d) remediate legitimate vulnerabilities within defined response times; (e) share vulnerability information with defined personnel; and (f) employ tools that can update the vulnerability database.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement b-3):** Severity must be based on CVSS scores (CVSS v3.1 preferred). Critical, High, Moderate, Low, and Informational classifications must be present in scan output.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Vulnerability scan results (last 30 days)
- Scan schedule and tool configuration
- Vulnerability remediation tracker (POA&M or ticket system)
- Scan tool update/signature verification

column1: RA-05 c: Are vulnerability scan results reviewed and analyzed by the appropriate team after every scan?
column8:
**Control:** RA-05 — Vulnerability Monitoring and Scanning

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
RA-05: (a) Monitor and scan for vulnerabilities at a defined frequency and when new vulnerabilities are identified; (b) employ vulnerability scanning tools that enumerate platforms, software flaws, and improper configurations; (c) analyze vulnerability scan reports; (d) remediate legitimate vulnerabilities within defined response times; (e) share vulnerability information with defined personnel; and (f) employ tools that can update the vulnerability database.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement c):** Scan results must be reviewed by the ISSO or security team within a reasonable time after scan completion. False positives must be documented with justification.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Vulnerability scan results (last 30 days)
- Scan schedule and tool configuration
- Vulnerability remediation tracker (POA&M or ticket system)
- Scan tool update/signature verification

column1: RA-05 d: Are critical and high vulnerabilities fixed within 30 days, moderate within 90 days, and low within 180 days?
column8:
**Control:** RA-05 — Vulnerability Monitoring and Scanning

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
RA-05: (a) Monitor and scan for vulnerabilities at a defined frequency and when new vulnerabilities are identified; (b) employ vulnerability scanning tools that enumerate platforms, software flaws, and improper configurations; (c) analyze vulnerability scan reports; (d) remediate legitimate vulnerabilities within defined response times; (e) share vulnerability information with defined personnel; and (f) employ tools that can update the vulnerability database.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement d):** [GovRAMP-defined] Response times = critical and high: 30 days; moderate: 90 days; low: 180 days. Exceptions require a POA&M with justification and AO acknowledgment.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Vulnerability scan results (last 30 days)
- Scan schedule and tool configuration
- Vulnerability remediation tracker (POA&M or ticket system)
- Scan tool update/signature verification

column1: RA-05 e: Are vulnerability scan results shared with the people responsible for remediating them?
column8:
**Control:** RA-05 — Vulnerability Monitoring and Scanning

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
RA-05: (a) Monitor and scan for vulnerabilities at a defined frequency and when new vulnerabilities are identified; (b) employ vulnerability scanning tools that enumerate platforms, software flaws, and improper configurations; (c) analyze vulnerability scan reports; (d) remediate legitimate vulnerabilities within defined response times; (e) share vulnerability information with defined personnel; and (f) employ tools that can update the vulnerability database.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement e):** [Assignment resolved] Personnel = ISSO and system owners/component owners responsible for the affected systems.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Vulnerability scan results (last 30 days)
- Scan schedule and tool configuration
- Vulnerability remediation tracker (POA&M or ticket system)
- Scan tool update/signature verification

column1: RA-05 f: Does your vulnerability scanning tool automatically update its vulnerability database before each scan?
column8:
**Control:** RA-05 — Vulnerability Monitoring and Scanning

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
RA-05: (a) Monitor and scan for vulnerabilities at a defined frequency and when new vulnerabilities are identified; (b) employ vulnerability scanning tools that enumerate platforms, software flaws, and improper configurations; (c) analyze vulnerability scan reports; (d) remediate legitimate vulnerabilities within defined response times; (e) share vulnerability information with defined personnel; and (f) employ tools that can update the vulnerability database.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement f):** Signature/plugin updates must be current at time of scan. Document the tool version and last update date in scan reports.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Vulnerability scan results (last 30 days)
- Scan schedule and tool configuration
- Vulnerability remediation tracker (POA&M or ticket system)
- Scan tool update/signature verification

column1: RA-05 (05): Are vulnerability scans run using authenticated (credentialed) access to get complete and accurate results?
column8:
**Control:** RA-05(05) — Privileged Access

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
RA-05(05): Implement privileged access authorization to defined system components for defined vulnerability scanning activities.

**GovRAMP Parameter:** [Assignment resolved] System components = all in-scope infrastructure and web applications. Scanning activities = all routine vulnerability scans. Credentialed (authenticated) scans are required — unauthenticated scans alone are not sufficient.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Scan configuration showing authenticated/credentialed scan settings
- Scan results showing authenticated scan data (not just external port scans)
- Scan service account or credential documentation

column1: SA-11 a: Does your development team have a documented plan for ongoing security testing as part of the development and deployment process?
column8:
**Control:** SA-11 — Developer Testing and Evaluation

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SA-11: Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to: (a) develop and implement a security and privacy assessment plan; (b) perform testing at a defined frequency and depth; (c) produce evidence of the assessment and results; (d) implement a verifiable flaw remediation process; and (e) correct identified flaws.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement a):** The security assessment plan must cover what is tested, how often, by whom, and how findings are tracked and remediated.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Security testing plan or test schedule
- Test results or scan output
- Flaw remediation tracker or ticketing records
- Evidence of retesting after fixes

column1: SA-11 b: Does your team perform security testing — including integration and regression tests — at each major release or significant change?
column8:
**Control:** SA-11 — Developer Testing and Evaluation

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SA-11: Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to: (a) develop and implement a security and privacy assessment plan; (b) perform testing at a defined frequency and depth; (c) produce evidence of the assessment and results; (d) implement a verifiable flaw remediation process; and (e) correct identified flaws.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement b):** [Assignment resolved] Testing types = integration and regression testing. Frequency = at each major release or significant system change. Depth and coverage = full in-scope system components.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Security testing plan or test schedule
- Test results or scan output
- Flaw remediation tracker or ticketing records
- Evidence of retesting after fixes

column1: SA-11 c: Are test results documented and retained as evidence of the testing that was performed?
column8:
**Control:** SA-11 — Developer Testing and Evaluation

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SA-11: Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to: (a) develop and implement a security and privacy assessment plan; (b) perform testing at a defined frequency and depth; (c) produce evidence of the assessment and results; (d) implement a verifiable flaw remediation process; and (e) correct identified flaws.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement c):** Test execution records, scan outputs, and results reports must be retained. This is the primary evidence that testing actually occurred — undocumented tests do not count.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Security testing plan or test schedule
- Test results or scan output
- Flaw remediation tracker or ticketing records
- Evidence of retesting after fixes

column1: SA-11 d: Is there a tracked, verifiable process for fixing security flaws found during testing?
column8:
**Control:** SA-11 — Developer Testing and Evaluation

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SA-11: Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to: (a) develop and implement a security and privacy assessment plan; (b) perform testing at a defined frequency and depth; (c) produce evidence of the assessment and results; (d) implement a verifiable flaw remediation process; and (e) correct identified flaws.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement d):** Flaw remediation must be tracked in a ticketing system with due dates, owners, and completion confirmation. A POA&M is required for accepted risks or deferred items.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Security testing plan or test schedule
- Test results or scan output
- Flaw remediation tracker or ticketing records
- Evidence of retesting after fixes

column1: SA-11 e: Are security flaws identified during testing actually fixed — and is there evidence of the fix?
column8:
**Control:** SA-11 — Developer Testing and Evaluation

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SA-11: Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to: (a) develop and implement a security and privacy assessment plan; (b) perform testing at a defined frequency and depth; (c) produce evidence of the assessment and results; (d) implement a verifiable flaw remediation process; and (e) correct identified flaws.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement e):** Closed tickets must reference the fix applied. Retesting after the fix is strongly recommended and may be required for critical/high findings.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Security testing plan or test schedule
- Test results or scan output
- Flaw remediation tracker or ticketing records
- Evidence of retesting after fixes

column1: SA-11 (01): Does your development pipeline include a static code analysis (SAST) tool that automatically scans code for security vulnerabilities before deployment?
column8:
**Control:** SA-11(01) — Static Code Analysis

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SA-11(01): Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to employ static code analysis tools to identify common flaws and document the results of the analysis.

**GovRAMP Parameter:** Static analysis must be integrated into the CI/CD pipeline. Recommended tools: Semgrep (free), GitHub CodeQL (free for public repos), Snyk, or SonarQube. Results must be documented and findings tracked to closure.

**Suggested evidence:**
- CI/CD pipeline configuration showing SAST step
- Most recent SAST scan report with findings
- Evidence that findings are tracked and remediated (tickets or POA&M)

column1: SC-02: Is the user-facing part of the system (what end users see and interact with) kept technically separate from the administrative and management functions?
column8:
**Control:** SC-02 — Separation of System and User Functionality

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SC-02: Separate user functionality, including user interface services, from system management functionality.

**GovRAMP Parameter:** End-user application functions and administrative/management functions must be technically separated — not just behind different menus. Separate access paths (e.g., AWS Console vs. application web portal), separate authentication domains, or separate network segments are acceptable implementations.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Architecture diagram showing logical separation of user and admin functions
- IAM configuration showing separate permissions for user vs. admin access
- Network diagram showing separate access paths

column1: SC-04: Does your system prevent information in shared resources (such as memory, storage, or network buffers) from leaking between users or tenants?
column8:
**Control:** SC-04 — Information in Shared System Resources

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SC-04: Prevent unauthorized and unintended information transfer via shared system resources.

**GovRAMP Parameter:** For cloud-hosted (AWS) systems, hypervisor-level isolation prevents information leakage between EC2 instances. This is inherited from AWS. Document the inheritance explicitly, and for any multi-tenant application layers, address how tenant data isolation is enforced in the application.

**Suggested evidence:**
- SSP narrative referencing AWS hypervisor isolation (inherited)
- Application-level tenant isolation documentation (if multi-tenant)
- AWS FedRAMP documentation reference for SC-04

column1: SC-07 a: Are all communications entering and leaving your system monitored and controlled at defined boundary points?
column8:
**Control:** SC-07 — Boundary Protection

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SC-07: (a) Monitor and control communications at the external managed interfaces to the system and at key internal managed interfaces; (b) implement subnetworks for publicly accessible system components that are physically or logically separated from internal networks; and (c) connect to external networks only through managed interfaces.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement a):** Boundary monitoring must cover all external interfaces (internet-facing) and key internal interfaces (e.g., between application tier and database tier). VPC flow logs, WAF, and security groups provide this in AWS.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Network architecture diagram showing boundary protection devices
- Security group and firewall rule exports
- WAF configuration screenshot
- VPC flow log or network monitoring evidence

column1: SC-07 b: Are publicly accessible system components (like web servers) logically separated from your internal private network?
column8:
**Control:** SC-07 — Boundary Protection

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SC-07: (a) Monitor and control communications at the external managed interfaces to the system and at key internal managed interfaces; (b) implement subnetworks for publicly accessible system components that are physically or logically separated from internal networks; and (c) connect to external networks only through managed interfaces.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement b):** [Assignment resolved] Separation type = logical separation using VPC subnets (public subnet for load balancer/web tier; private subnet for application and database tiers). Public and private subnets must be in separate security groups with controlled access.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Network architecture diagram showing boundary protection devices
- Security group and firewall rule exports
- WAF configuration screenshot
- VPC flow log or network monitoring evidence

column1: SC-07 c: Does all traffic to and from external networks pass through a controlled, managed boundary device?
column8:
**Control:** SC-07 — Boundary Protection

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SC-07: (a) Monitor and control communications at the external managed interfaces to the system and at key internal managed interfaces; (b) implement subnetworks for publicly accessible system components that are physically or logically separated from internal networks; and (c) connect to external networks only through managed interfaces.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement c):** No direct, unmanaged connections to external networks are allowed. All traffic must pass through security groups, NAT gateways, or other managed boundary devices. Outbound internet access for private resources must go through a NAT gateway, not a direct internet gateway.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Network architecture diagram showing boundary protection devices
- Security group and firewall rule exports
- WAF configuration screenshot
- VPC flow log or network monitoring evidence

column1: SC-07 (03): Is the number of external network connections to your system limited to the minimum necessary?
column8:
**Control:** SC-07(03) — Access Points

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SC-07(03): Limit the number of external network connections to the system.

**GovRAMP Parameter:** Only the minimum required external connections should exist. Unused ports, protocols, and services must be disabled. The number of internet-facing entry points must be documented and justified.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Network diagram showing all external connection points
- Security group rules showing only necessary ports are open
- Justification for each external-facing entry point

column1: SC-08: Is all data transmitted to and from your system protected to prevent unauthorized viewing or tampering in transit?
column8:
**Control:** SC-08 — Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SC-08: Protect the confidentiality and/or integrity of transmitted information.

**GovRAMP Parameter:** [Assignment resolved] Both confidentiality and integrity of transmitted information must be protected. TLS 1.2 or higher is required for all web-based communications. HTTP (unencrypted) must be disabled or redirected to HTTPS.

**Suggested evidence:**
- SSL/TLS configuration evidence (SSL Labs A-grade or equivalent)
- Screenshot confirming HTTP redirects to HTTPS (no cleartext access)
- Certificate management documentation

column1: SC-08: Is all data transmitted to and from your system protected to prevent unauthorized viewing or tampering in transit?
column8:
**Control:** SC-08 — Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SC-08: Protect the confidentiality and/or integrity of transmitted information.

**GovRAMP Parameter:** [Assignment resolved] Both confidentiality and integrity of transmitted information must be protected. TLS 1.2 or higher is required for all web-based communications. HTTP (unencrypted) must be disabled or redirected to HTTPS.

**Suggested evidence:**
- SSL/TLS configuration evidence (SSL Labs A-grade or equivalent)
- Screenshot confirming HTTP redirects to HTTPS (no cleartext access)
- Certificate management documentation

column1: SC-12: Does your organization have a documented key management process that governs how cryptographic keys are generated, distributed, stored, accessed, and destroyed?
column8:
**Control:** SC-12 — Cryptographic Key Establishment and Management

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SC-12: Establish and manage cryptographic keys when cryptography is employed within the system in accordance with the following key management requirements: defined requirements for key generation, distribution, storage, access, and destruction.

**GovRAMP Parameter:** [Assignment resolved] Key management requirements = NIST SP 800-57 guidance and FIPS 140-3 validated cryptographic modules. For AWS: AWS KMS handles key lifecycle using FIPS 140-3 validated HSMs. Document: how keys are generated (KMS), rotation schedule (annual or automatic), access controls (IAM policy), and deletion process (KMS key deletion with 7-30 day waiting period).

**Suggested evidence:**
- AWS KMS key configuration screenshot showing rotation enabled
- Key management policy document (1–2 pages)
- IAM policy showing who can access/manage keys
- Key inventory listing all in-scope KMS keys

column1: SC-13 a: Has your organization identified and documented all the specific uses of cryptography in your system?
column8:
**Control:** SC-13 — Cryptographic Protection

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SC-13: (a) Determine the defined cryptographic uses; and (b) implement the following types of cryptography required for each specified cryptographic use.

**GovRAMP Parameter:** [Assignment resolved] Cryptographic uses = data at rest, data in transit, and authentication. Each use must be explicitly identified.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Encryption policy or data protection policy
- SSP section documenting all cryptographic uses
- System architecture diagram annotated with encryption points

column1: SC-13 b: Does your organization use FIPS-validated encryption — AES-256 for data at rest and TLS 1.2+ for data in transit?
column8:
**Control:** SC-13 — Cryptographic Protection

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SC-13(b): Implement the required types of cryptography for each specified cryptographic use.

**GovRAMP Parameter:** [Assignment resolved] Types of cryptography = AES-256 (or equivalent FIPS 140-3 validated algorithm) for data at rest; TLS 1.2 or higher for data in transit; FIPS 140-3 validated modules wherever cryptography is implemented.

**Suggested evidence:**
- AWS S3/RDS encryption configuration showing AES-256 or SSE-KMS
- TLS configuration evidence showing TLS 1.2+ in use
- AWS KMS documentation showing FIPS 140-3 validated HSM usage

column1: SC-20 a: Does your DNS infrastructure provide origin authentication and data integrity verification for the DNS records it serves?
column8:
**Control:** SC-20 — Secure Name/Address Resolution Service (Authoritative Source)

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SC-20: (a) Provide additional data origin authentication and integrity verification artifacts along with the authoritative name resolution data the system returns in response to external name/address resolution queries; and (b) provide the means to indicate the security status of child zones and enable verification of a chain of trust among parent and child domains.

**GovRAMP Parameter:** DNSSEC implementation is the primary mechanism satisfying this control. For AWS Route 53: Route 53 supports DNSSEC signing for public hosted zones. Document whether DNSSEC is enabled and the chain of trust configuration.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Route 53 DNSSEC configuration screenshot
- DNSSEC validation test result (e.g., DNSVIZ or dnssec-analyzer)
- AWS Route 53 FedRAMP inheritance documentation

column1: SC-20 b: If your DNS infrastructure serves child zones, can it communicate the security status of those zones and support a chain of trust?
column8:
**Control:** SC-20 — Secure Name/Address Resolution Service (Authoritative Source)

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
See SC-20.

**GovRAMP Parameter:** For AWS Route 53 with DNSSEC enabled, the chain of trust between parent and child zones is maintained automatically. Document the DNSSEC trust chain configuration.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Route 53 DNSSEC zone signing key (ZSK) and key signing key (KSK) configuration
- DNSSEC chain of trust validation result

column1: SC-21: Does your system validate the authenticity and integrity of DNS responses it receives before using them?
column8:
**Control:** SC-21 — Secure Name/Address Resolution Service (Recursive or Caching Resolver)

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SC-21: Request and perform data origin authentication and data integrity verification on the name/address resolution responses the system receives from authoritative sources.

**GovRAMP Parameter:** For AWS-hosted systems using the AWS VPC DNS resolver: this is inherited from AWS infrastructure. Document the inheritance explicitly.

**Suggested evidence:**
- AWS VPC DNS resolver configuration
- AWS FedRAMP documentation for SC-21 inheritance

column1: SC-28: Is all sensitive data stored in your system encrypted to protect both its confidentiality and integrity at rest?
column8:
**Control:** SC-28 — Protection of Information at Rest

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SC-28: Protect the confidentiality and integrity of the following information at rest: all organization-defined sensitive and CUI information stored within the system boundary.

**GovRAMP Parameter:** [Assignment resolved] Both confidentiality AND integrity protection are required. Information at rest = all data stored in databases, object storage (S3), EBS volumes, backup snapshots, and application data stores within the authorization boundary. Encryption must use FIPS 140-3 validated modules (e.g., AWS KMS with AES-256).

**Important notes:**
- For AWS-hosted systems: verify that encryption is explicitly configured — it is not always enabled by default on all services. Check RDS, S3, EBS, and EFS.
- Encryption must be in accordance with SC-13 (FIPS-validated algorithms).
- Customer-managed keys (CMKs) via AWS KMS are preferred for GovRAMP.

**Suggested evidence:**
- RDS encryption configuration screenshot showing encryption enabled with KMS key
- S3 bucket default encryption configuration (SSE-KMS or SSE-S3)
- EBS volume encryption configuration
- AWS KMS key inventory showing CMKs in use
- Data encryption policy document

column1: SI-02 a: Does your organization identify, track, and fix system flaws — including security vulnerabilities — on an ongoing basis?
column8:
**Control:** SI-02 — Flaw Remediation

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SI-02: (a) Identify, report, and correct system flaws; (b) test software and firmware updates for effectiveness and potential side effects before installation; (c) install security-relevant software and firmware updates within a defined time period of release; and (d) incorporate flaw remediation into the organizational configuration management process.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement a):** Flaws must be identified through vulnerability scanning, SIEM alerts, vendor advisories, and penetration testing. Each finding must be tracked to closure.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Vulnerability scan results showing identified flaws
- Patch management records showing patching timeline
- Change management tickets for patches
- POA&M for any delayed remediation items

column1: SI-02 b: Are software and firmware updates tested for compatibility and unintended side effects before being deployed to production?
column8:
**Control:** SI-02 — Flaw Remediation

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SI-02: (a) Identify, report, and correct system flaws; (b) test software and firmware updates for effectiveness and potential side effects before installation; (c) install security-relevant software and firmware updates within a defined time period of release; and (d) incorporate flaw remediation into the organizational configuration management process.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement b):** Testing should occur in a dev/staging environment that mirrors production. Test results or a defined approval checklist must be documented before production deployment.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Vulnerability scan results showing identified flaws
- Patch management records showing patching timeline
- Change management tickets for patches
- POA&M for any delayed remediation items

column1: SI-02 c: Are critical and high security patches applied within 30 days of release, and moderate patches within 90 days?
column8:
**Control:** SI-02 — Flaw Remediation

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SI-02: (a) Identify, report, and correct system flaws; (b) test software and firmware updates for effectiveness and potential side effects before installation; (c) install security-relevant software and firmware updates within a defined time period of release; and (d) incorporate flaw remediation into the organizational configuration management process.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement c):** [GovRAMP-defined] Patching SLAs = critical and high: within 30 days of patch availability; moderate: within 90 days; low: within 180 days. Delays require a POA&M entry.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Vulnerability scan results showing identified flaws
- Patch management records showing patching timeline
- Change management tickets for patches
- POA&M for any delayed remediation items

column1: SI-02 d: Is the patch and flaw remediation process integrated with your change management workflow?
column8:
**Control:** SI-02 — Flaw Remediation

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SI-02: (a) Identify, report, and correct system flaws; (b) test software and firmware updates for effectiveness and potential side effects before installation; (c) install security-relevant software and firmware updates within a defined time period of release; and (d) incorporate flaw remediation into the organizational configuration management process.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement d):** Every patch must go through the change management process — no ad-hoc patching in production without a change ticket. Patching must appear in the CM plan as a defined configuration-controlled activity.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Vulnerability scan results showing identified flaws
- Patch management records showing patching timeline
- Change management tickets for patches
- POA&M for any delayed remediation items

column1: SI-02 (02): Does an automated tool check monthly whether all system components have the latest security patches installed?
column8:
**Control:** SI-02(02) — Automated Flaw Remediation Status

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SI-02(02): Determine if system components have applicable security-relevant software and firmware updates installed using defined automated mechanisms at a defined frequency.

**GovRAMP Parameter:** [Assignment resolved] Automated mechanisms = patch management tool or vulnerability scanner (e.g., Tenable, Qualys, CrowdStrike Spotlight, AWS Inspector). Frequency = monthly.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Monthly automated patch compliance report
- Scan tool configuration showing automated monthly scans
- Dashboard screenshot showing patch status across all components

column1: SI-03 a: Is malware detection and prevention software deployed at all system entry and exit points — including endpoints and network boundaries?
column8:
**Control:** SI-03 — Malicious Code Protection

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SI-03: (a) Implement malicious code protection mechanisms at system entry and exit points; (b) automatically update malicious code protection mechanisms; (c-1) perform periodic scans and real-time scans of external-source files; (c-2) take action and alert defined personnel when malicious code is detected; and (d) address false positives and their impact on system availability.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement a):** [Assignment resolved] Protection mechanisms = signature-based and non-signature-based (behavioral) detection. Must cover both endpoints (CrowdStrike, Defender) and network entry/exit points (WAF, email filtering).

**Suggested evidence:**
- Endpoint protection tool configuration (e.g., CrowdStrike, Defender, Sentinel One)
- Real-time protection and scan policy screenshots
- Recent scan results or malware detection logs
- Automatic update configuration confirmation

column1: SI-03 b: Are malware protection signatures and threat intelligence updated automatically as soon as new updates are available?
column8:
**Control:** SI-03 — Malicious Code Protection

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SI-03: (a) Implement malicious code protection mechanisms at system entry and exit points; (b) automatically update malicious code protection mechanisms; (c-1) perform periodic scans and real-time scans of external-source files; (c-2) take action and alert defined personnel when malicious code is detected; and (d) address false positives and their impact on system availability.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement b):** Automatic updates must be enabled. Manual-only updates are not acceptable. Document the update frequency and confirm that the tool is current at time of assessment.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Endpoint protection tool configuration (e.g., CrowdStrike, Defender, Sentinel One)
- Real-time protection and scan policy screenshots
- Recent scan results or malware detection logs
- Automatic update configuration confirmation

column1: SI-03 c-1: Are weekly automated scans performed across the system, and are files from external sources scanned in real time when they are downloaded or opened?
column8:
**Control:** SI-03 — Malicious Code Protection

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SI-03: (a) Implement malicious code protection mechanisms at system entry and exit points; (b) automatically update malicious code protection mechanisms; (c-1) perform periodic scans and real-time scans of external-source files; (c-2) take action and alert defined personnel when malicious code is detected; and (d) address false positives and their impact on system availability.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement c-1):** [Assignment resolved] Scan frequency = weekly full scans. Real-time scanning at = endpoints and network entry/exit points. All files from external sources must be scanned before execution.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Endpoint protection tool configuration (e.g., CrowdStrike, Defender, Sentinel One)
- Real-time protection and scan policy screenshots
- Recent scan results or malware detection logs
- Automatic update configuration confirmation

column1: SI-03 c-2: When malware is detected, is it automatically quarantined and does an alert go to the ISSO and security team?
column8:
**Control:** SI-03 — Malicious Code Protection

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SI-03: (a) Implement malicious code protection mechanisms at system entry and exit points; (b) automatically update malicious code protection mechanisms; (c-1) perform periodic scans and real-time scans of external-source files; (c-2) take action and alert defined personnel when malicious code is detected; and (d) address false positives and their impact on system availability.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement c-2):** [Assignment resolved] Actions = quarantine malicious code. Alert recipients = ISSO and security operations team. Alerts must be automated — manual detection and notification alone is not sufficient.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Endpoint protection tool configuration (e.g., CrowdStrike, Defender, Sentinel One)
- Real-time protection and scan policy screenshots
- Recent scan results or malware detection logs
- Automatic update configuration confirmation

column1: SI-03 d: Does your team have a process for reviewing and addressing false positive detections to avoid unnecessary disruption to operations?
column8:
**Control:** SI-03 — Malicious Code Protection

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SI-03: (a) Implement malicious code protection mechanisms at system entry and exit points; (b) automatically update malicious code protection mechanisms; (c-1) perform periodic scans and real-time scans of external-source files; (c-2) take action and alert defined personnel when malicious code is detected; and (d) address false positives and their impact on system availability.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement d):** A false positive review process must exist. False positives must be documented, reviewed, and added to an exclusion list with justification and ISSO approval. The impact of false positives on system availability must be tracked.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Endpoint protection tool configuration (e.g., CrowdStrike, Defender, Sentinel One)
- Real-time protection and scan policy screenshots
- Recent scan results or malware detection logs
- Automatic update configuration confirmation

column1: SI-04 a-1: Is your system monitored to detect attacks, indicators of compromise, and other suspicious activity across all system layers?
column8:
**Control:** SI-04 — System Monitoring

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SI-04: (a) Monitor the system to detect: (1) attacks and indicators of potential attacks; (2) unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; (b) identify unauthorized use through defined techniques; (c) deploy monitoring at strategic and ad hoc locations; (d) analyze detected events and anomalies; (e) adjust monitoring level when risk changes; (f) obtain legal opinion regarding monitoring activities; and (g) provide monitoring information to defined personnel at defined frequency.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement a-1):** [Assignment resolved] Monitoring objectives = detect attacks, indicators of compromise (IOCs), anomalous behavior, and policy violations across network, host, and application layers.

**Suggested evidence:**
- SIEM configuration screenshot (CloudWatch, Security Hub, Splunk, or equivalent)
- GuardDuty or IDS/IPS configuration
- Monitoring objectives documentation
- Sample alert or event showing monitoring in action

column1: SI-04 a-2: Does your monitoring detect unauthorized connections — whether local, remote, or over the network?
column8:
**Control:** SI-04 — System Monitoring

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SI-04: (a) Monitor the system to detect: (1) attacks and indicators of potential attacks; (2) unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; (b) identify unauthorized use through defined techniques; (c) deploy monitoring at strategic and ad hoc locations; (d) analyze detected events and anomalies; (e) adjust monitoring level when risk changes; (f) obtain legal opinion regarding monitoring activities; and (g) provide monitoring information to defined personnel at defined frequency.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement a-2):** VPC flow logs, GuardDuty, and CloudTrail must collectively detect unauthorized network connections, unexpected API calls, and remote access attempts.

**Suggested evidence:**
- SIEM configuration screenshot (CloudWatch, Security Hub, Splunk, or equivalent)
- GuardDuty or IDS/IPS configuration
- Monitoring objectives documentation
- Sample alert or event showing monitoring in action

column1: SI-04 b: Does your organization use specific techniques — such as SIEM correlation rules or anomaly detection — to identify unauthorized use of the system?
column8:
**Control:** SI-04 — System Monitoring

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SI-04: (a) Monitor the system to detect: (1) attacks and indicators of potential attacks; (2) unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; (b) identify unauthorized use through defined techniques; (c) deploy monitoring at strategic and ad hoc locations; (d) analyze detected events and anomalies; (e) adjust monitoring level when risk changes; (f) obtain legal opinion regarding monitoring activities; and (g) provide monitoring information to defined personnel at defined frequency.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement b):** [Assignment resolved] Techniques = SIEM correlation rules, behavioral anomaly detection (e.g., GuardDuty ML-based threat detection), and log analysis. These must be actively tuned and reviewed.

**Suggested evidence:**
- SIEM configuration screenshot (CloudWatch, Security Hub, Splunk, or equivalent)
- GuardDuty or IDS/IPS configuration
- Monitoring objectives documentation
- Sample alert or event showing monitoring in action

column1: SI-04 c-1: Are monitoring tools deployed at the most important points in the system — the places most likely to reveal attacks?
column8:
**Control:** SI-04 — System Monitoring

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SI-04: (a) Monitor the system to detect: (1) attacks and indicators of potential attacks; (2) unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; (b) identify unauthorized use through defined techniques; (c) deploy monitoring at strategic and ad hoc locations; (d) analyze detected events and anomalies; (e) adjust monitoring level when risk changes; (f) obtain legal opinion regarding monitoring activities; and (g) provide monitoring information to defined personnel at defined frequency.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement c-1):** Strategic monitoring locations include: network boundary (VPC flow logs, WAF), authentication events (CloudTrail), privilege use (CloudTrail), and endpoint behavior (CrowdStrike or equivalent).

**Suggested evidence:**
- SIEM configuration screenshot (CloudWatch, Security Hub, Splunk, or equivalent)
- GuardDuty or IDS/IPS configuration
- Monitoring objectives documentation
- Sample alert or event showing monitoring in action

column1: SI-04 c-2: Can monitoring be targeted at specific transactions or behaviors of interest when needed for an investigation?
column8:
**Control:** SI-04 — System Monitoring

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SI-04: (a) Monitor the system to detect: (1) attacks and indicators of potential attacks; (2) unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; (b) identify unauthorized use through defined techniques; (c) deploy monitoring at strategic and ad hoc locations; (d) analyze detected events and anomalies; (e) adjust monitoring level when risk changes; (f) obtain legal opinion regarding monitoring activities; and (g) provide monitoring information to defined personnel at defined frequency.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement c-2):** Ad hoc monitoring capability must exist — the ability to focus monitoring on a specific user, resource, or behavior during an investigation without deploying new infrastructure.

**Suggested evidence:**
- SIEM configuration screenshot (CloudWatch, Security Hub, Splunk, or equivalent)
- GuardDuty or IDS/IPS configuration
- Monitoring objectives documentation
- Sample alert or event showing monitoring in action

column1: SI-04 d: Are detected events and anomalies analyzed to determine whether they represent a real threat?
column8:
**Control:** SI-04 — System Monitoring

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SI-04: (a) Monitor the system to detect: (1) attacks and indicators of potential attacks; (2) unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; (b) identify unauthorized use through defined techniques; (c) deploy monitoring at strategic and ad hoc locations; (d) analyze detected events and anomalies; (e) adjust monitoring level when risk changes; (f) obtain legal opinion regarding monitoring activities; and (g) provide monitoring information to defined personnel at defined frequency.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement d):** Event analysis must be performed by a qualified person (ISSO, security analyst) at defined intervals. Automated triage is acceptable for initial classification, but human review is required for escalated events.

**Suggested evidence:**
- SIEM configuration screenshot (CloudWatch, Security Hub, Splunk, or equivalent)
- GuardDuty or IDS/IPS configuration
- Monitoring objectives documentation
- Sample alert or event showing monitoring in action

column1: SI-04 e: When the threat level increases, does your team increase the level of system monitoring accordingly?
column8:
**Control:** SI-04 — System Monitoring

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SI-04: (a) Monitor the system to detect: (1) attacks and indicators of potential attacks; (2) unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; (b) identify unauthorized use through defined techniques; (c) deploy monitoring at strategic and ad hoc locations; (d) analyze detected events and anomalies; (e) adjust monitoring level when risk changes; (f) obtain legal opinion regarding monitoring activities; and (g) provide monitoring information to defined personnel at defined frequency.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement e):** A defined process must exist for escalating monitoring intensity in response to increased threat levels (e.g., during a US-CERT advisory, active incident, or elevated CISA threat level). Evidence could be a WAF rule update, additional CloudWatch alarms, or a Jira ticket documenting the action taken.

**Suggested evidence:**
- SIEM configuration screenshot (CloudWatch, Security Hub, Splunk, or equivalent)
- GuardDuty or IDS/IPS configuration
- Monitoring objectives documentation
- Sample alert or event showing monitoring in action

column1: SI-04 f: Has legal counsel reviewed and signed off that your system monitoring activities are legally authorized?
column8:
**Control:** SI-04 — System Monitoring

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SI-04: (a) Monitor the system to detect: (1) attacks and indicators of potential attacks; (2) unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; (b) identify unauthorized use through defined techniques; (c) deploy monitoring at strategic and ad hoc locations; (d) analyze detected events and anomalies; (e) adjust monitoring level when risk changes; (f) obtain legal opinion regarding monitoring activities; and (g) provide monitoring information to defined personnel at defined frequency.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement f):** A legal opinion (memo, signed policy, or attorney letter) confirming that employee and user monitoring is legally authorized must be on file. This is a common gap — engage legal counsel early. The opinion must cover all monitoring in scope: network, endpoint, and user activity monitoring.

**Suggested evidence:**
- SIEM configuration screenshot (CloudWatch, Security Hub, Splunk, or equivalent)
- GuardDuty or IDS/IPS configuration
- Monitoring objectives documentation
- Sample alert or event showing monitoring in action

column1: SI-04 g: Are monitoring reports and security alerts shared with the ISSO and System Owner on a regular basis and immediately after significant events?
column8:
**Control:** SI-04 — System Monitoring

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SI-04: (a) Monitor the system to detect: (1) attacks and indicators of potential attacks; (2) unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; (b) identify unauthorized use through defined techniques; (c) deploy monitoring at strategic and ad hoc locations; (d) analyze detected events and anomalies; (e) adjust monitoring level when risk changes; (f) obtain legal opinion regarding monitoring activities; and (g) provide monitoring information to defined personnel at defined frequency.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement g):** [Assignment resolved] Monitoring information = SIEM alerts, incident summaries, and anomaly reports. Personnel = ISSO and System Owner. Frequency = as needed and within 24 hours of a significant security event.

**Suggested evidence:**
- SIEM configuration screenshot (CloudWatch, Security Hub, Splunk, or equivalent)
- GuardDuty or IDS/IPS configuration
- Monitoring objectives documentation
- Sample alert or event showing monitoring in action

column1: SI-04 (04): Has your organization defined what 'unusual' or 'unauthorized' network traffic looks like — and is that traffic actively monitored?
column8:
**Control:** SI-04(04) — Inbound and Outbound Communications Traffic

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SI-04(04): (a) Determine criteria for unusual or unauthorized activities or conditions for inbound and outbound communications traffic; and (b) monitor inbound and outbound communications traffic at a defined frequency for defined unusual or unauthorized activities or conditions.

**GovRAMP Parameter:** [Assignment resolved] (a) Criteria = defined in SIEM rules and threat detection policies (e.g., connections to known malicious IPs, unusual data transfer volumes, connections on non-standard ports). (b) Monitoring frequency = continuously via automated SIEM/IDS (or at minimum daily review).

**Suggested evidence:**
- SIEM/IDS rule configuration showing inbound and outbound traffic criteria
- GuardDuty or WAF configuration screenshot showing traffic monitoring
- Sample alert from anomalous traffic detection

column1: SI-07 a: Does your organization use a file integrity monitoring tool to detect unauthorized changes to critical system files, executables, or configuration?
column8:
**Control:** SI-07 — Software, Firmware, and Information Integrity

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SI-07: (a) Employ integrity verification tools to detect unauthorized changes to defined software, firmware, and information; and (b) take defined actions when unauthorized changes to the software, firmware, and information are detected.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement a):** [Assignment resolved] Software, firmware, and information subject to integrity monitoring = critical system executables, configuration files, boot sectors, and security-relevant application files. Tools: CrowdStrike FIM, AWS Config rules, AIDE, or Tripwire.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Integrity monitoring tool configuration (CrowdStrike FIM, AWS Config, AIDE, Tripwire)
- Recent integrity check results or FIM alerts
- Documented response procedure for integrity violations

column1: SI-07 b: When an unauthorized change to a critical file is detected, is the ISSO alerted and is the affected system quarantined or restored from backup?
column8:
**Control:** SI-07 — Software, Firmware, and Information Integrity

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SI-07: (a) Employ integrity verification tools to detect unauthorized changes to defined software, firmware, and information; and (b) take defined actions when unauthorized changes to the software, firmware, and information are detected.

**GovRAMP Parameter (Requirement b):** [Assignment resolved] Actions = alert the ISSO immediately, quarantine the affected system if actively compromised, and restore from a known-good backup or validated snapshot.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Integrity monitoring tool configuration (CrowdStrike FIM, AWS Config, AIDE, Tripwire)
- Recent integrity check results or FIM alerts
- Documented response procedure for integrity violations

column1: SI-07 (07): Are unauthorized changes to security-relevant configurations and software automatically fed into the incident response process?
column8:
**Control:** SI-07(07) — Integration of Detection and Response

**NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control Language:**
SI-07(07): Incorporate the detection of defined unauthorized changes into the organizational incident response capability.

**GovRAMP Parameter:** [Assignment resolved] Security-relevant changes = unauthorized software installation, privilege escalation, changes to security group rules, IAM policy modifications, and changes to security-relevant configuration files. Detections must automatically trigger an IR ticket or SIEM alert.

**Suggested evidence:**
- Integration between FIM/AWS Config and SIEM or ticketing system
- Example alert showing unauthorized change detected and IR ticket created
- IRP section documenting FIM-triggered incident response
